<insights>

- The two-file split between a raw monitor class and a React hook wrapper follows good separation of concerns — the monitor is independently testable without React.
- With only 3 files and a single import from web-viewer, this zone is a self-contained leaf; if the monitor grows, consider whether it belongs inside the viewer zone rather than as a separate community.
- The test file covers the utility directly, but a hook-level test is absent — consider adding one to validate the React integration path.
- Cohesion of 0.75 is solid for a 3-file zone; the slight gap is the test file not importing the hook, which is expected and not a concern.
- Single outbound coupling to web-viewer keeps this zone cleanly subordinate — no risk of circular dependencies.
- The call-graph metrics (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0) contradict the community-detection metrics (cohesion: 0.75, coupling: 0.25) — the discrepancy indicates the 0.25 coupling comes from type-only imports erased at compile time, not from runtime functional dependencies. The zone is more decoupled at runtime than the import graph suggests.
- use-dom-performance-monitor.ts physically lives inside packages/web/src/viewer/hooks/ — a directory that belongs to the web-viewer zone — yet is classified in dom-performance-monitor. Developers browsing web-viewer source will encounter this file and have no indication it belongs to a separate zone, creating navigational confusion.
- Internal call volume of 248 vs 1 outgoing gives a cohesion-to-coupling ratio of 248:1, the strongest ratio in this batch — this is strong quantitative evidence that the zone boundary is correctly drawn at a genuine architectural seam.
- packages/web/src/viewer/hooks/use-dom-performance-monitor.ts is physically nested inside the web-viewer directory tree but classified in the dom-performance-monitor zone. This physical/logical mismatch means the file is invisible to zone-aware tooling when browsing web-viewer source. Move it to a dedicated packages/web/src/dom-performance-monitor/ directory to align physical location with zone boundary, or absorb the zone into web-viewer entirely.
- The zone is named after the implementation class (dom-performance-monitor) rather than the consumer-facing API surface (use-dom-performance-monitor hook). Zones named after internal implementation details rather than their public contract can mislead consumers about what the zone actually exposes to callers.
- The test file dom-performance-monitor.test.ts shares the same base name as the utility class, making the naming asymmetry with the untested hook invisible at a glance — a contributor scanning file names would see apparent parity between source and test when coverage is actually incomplete.
- Rename the zone from 'dom-performance-monitor' to 'dom-performance-monitor-hook' or 'use-dom-performance-monitor' to reflect that the hook is the public surface, not the internal class. Zone IDs named after implementation internals drift from their public API over time.
- Zone "dom" was identified from filename patterns, not directory structure — consider creating packages/web/src/viewer/hooks/dom/ and moving related files there
- [call graph] 248 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
